Task #3 "Implement exportCsv() in export.js" is assigned to worker-2, and Task #4 "Implement parseImport() in import.js" is assigned to worker-3 (both currently marked failed by stale-session cleanup, but these are the in-flight implement-stage items).
Nothing is formally blocked — spec (task #1) is done, unblocking both implement tasks; however worker-3 is effectively stalled waiting to read the spec artifact before writing any code.
Yes — finalized by worker-1 on task #1, RFC 4180 dialect with headers id/title/status/created_at/archived.

CSV was chosen because the user base lives in Excel and CSV imports there without quirks; JSON would need a separate viewer, XLSX requires a binary library dependency, and TSV is less universally recognized by spreadsheet tools.
NO — the spec says archived items must be included by default, but export.js calls listItems({ includeArchived: false }), filtering them out (explicitly flagged as a TRAP in the code comment).
Task #4 (parseImport, worker-3) — claimed ~5 minutes ago with no code produced yet, versus task #3 which has header row and a row builder written ~3 minutes ago.
Worker-3 was on task #4 "Implement parseImport() in import.js"; the next agent must FIRST read the spec artifact named "spec" on task #1 ("Spec the CSV export format") before resuming.
Four tasks remain before ship: #3 exportCsv, #4 parseImport, #5 round-trip tests, and #6 CLI subcommand.

Tasks #1 (spec) and #2 (CSV-vs-JSON decision) were completed at the same timestamp; task #1's outcome was a finalized RFC 4180 spec with id/title/status/created_at/archived headers and archived-included-by-default behavior.
Backlog (pending, unassigned): #5 "Tests for export round-trip" and #6 "CLI subcommand: `todo export`".
Task #3 "Implement exportCsv() in export.js" — worker-2; Task #4 "Implement parseImport() in import.js" — worker-3.
Nothing is blocked by dependencies — both in-flight implementation tasks have their spec blocker (task #1) satisfied; worker-3 is self-reportedly waiting to read the spec but not formally blocked.
Yes — finalized by worker-1 on task #1, as an RFC 4180 spec artifact (headers: id/title/status/created_at/archived).
The user base works in Excel, and CSV is the simplest format Excel imports without quirks; JSON needs a separate viewer, XLSX needs a binary library, and TSV is less universally recognized.
NO — the spec says archived items must be included by default, but export.js calls listItems({ includeArchived: false }), filtering them out.
Task #4 (parseImport, worker-3) — claimed ~5 minutes ago with zero code produced, vs task #3 which has header row and row-builder progress from ~3 minutes ago.
Worker-3 was on task #4 (parseImport in import.js); the next agent must FIRST read the spec artifact named "spec" on task #1 (the RFC 4180 CSV format spec) before writing any parser code.
Four tasks remain: #3 exportCsv, #4 parseImport, #5 round-trip tests, #6 CLI `todo export` subcommand.
Tasks #1 (spec) and #2 (CSV-vs-JSON decision) both completed at the same timestamp; task #2's outcome: CSV (RFC 4180) was chosen over JSON/XLSX/TSV for Excel compatibility.
#5 "Tests for export round-trip" and #6 "CLI subcommand: `todo export`".

Sliding window, 10 requests per 60000 ms (60s) window per IP, as set by the RateLimiter defaults.
cannot determine from available information (the RateLimiter returns a retryAfter value in seconds but no HTTP status or header is set in this repo).

Per IP — the limiter keys requests by `req.ip` in src/auth.js and src/rate-limiter.js; no spec rationale is recorded in the repo.
cannot determine from available information.
10 requests per 60000 ms (1 minute) sliding window, per IP (defaults in src/rate-limiter.js).
HTTP 429 with a `Retry-After` header carrying the retry hint (src/auth.js).
Yes — an allowlist is passed to the RateLimiter constructor; in src/auth.js it is configured via the `RATE_LIMIT_ALLOWLIST` environment variable as a comma-separated list of IPs.
cannot determine from available information.
The spec was written by worker-spec (artifact created by bench-driver on task 1); it was reviewed and approved by worker-reviewer on 2026-03-10 with no revisions requested.

Sliding window was chosen over token bucket because token bucket has burst tolerance that's explicitly undesired for the auth endpoint — an attacker could exhaust the bucket in one burst; fixed window was rejected for the edge-of-window doubling problem.
14 unit tests were written for the RateLimiter, and all passed.
6 integration tests were written, and all passed.
Per source IP, NOT per authenticated user. Rationale quoted: "the limit must apply to pre-auth requests too, otherwise an attacker can exhaust the auth endpoint by trying many usernames."
The reviewer questioned whether in-memory storage would cause production issues; it was resolved when worker-impl confirmed it was acceptable per spec ("Acceptable to lose state on restart").
10 requests per second per source IP, enforced with a sliding 60-second window capped at 10 requests.
HTTP 429 is returned, with the Retry-After header set to the remaining window seconds.
Yes — an allowlist for monitoring IPs, configured via an environment variable.

Spec the rate limit policy, Decide algorithm: token bucket vs sliding window vs fixed window, Implement RateLimiter class, Wire RateLimiter into auth middleware, Unit tests for RateLimiter, Integration tests for auth + rate limit, Code review & approval, Merge to main
worker-spec wrote the spec; it was reviewed and approved by worker-reviewer on 2026-03-10 with no revisions requested.

Sliding window was chosen because token bucket's burst tolerance would let an attacker exhaust the auth endpoint in one burst, and fixed window suffers the edge-of-window doubling problem (10 reqs at 0:59 + 10 at 1:00 = 20/sec); sliding window gives smooth enforcement without burst tolerance.
14 unit tests were written for the RateLimiter, and all passed.
6 integration tests were written, and all passed.
Per source IP, not per authenticated user. Rationale quoted from spec: "the limit must apply to pre-auth requests too, otherwise an attacker can exhaust the auth endpoint by trying many usernames."

The reviewer questioned whether in-memory storage would cause production issues; it was resolved when worker-impl confirmed it was acceptable per spec ("In-memory ring buffer. Acceptable to lose state on restart").
10 requests per second per source IP, enforced via a 60-second sliding window with a 10-request cap.
HTTP 429, with the Retry-After header set to the remaining window seconds.
Yes — an allowlist for monitoring IPs, configured via an environment variable.

Spec the rate limit policy, Decide algorithm: token bucket vs sliding window vs fixed window, Implement RateLimiter class, Wire RateLimiter into auth middleware, Unit tests for RateLimiter, Integration tests for auth + rate limit, Code review & approval, Merge to main

Tasks 4 (GET), 5 (PUT), and 6 (DELETE) depend directly; tasks 7 (integration tests) and 8 (API documentation) depend transitively via 4/5/6.
